steps.title: Recognize the complementary angle relationship
steps.content: In a right triangle, the two non-right angles must sum to 90° (since all three angles sum to 180° and one is already 90°). The problem confirms $R + S = 90°$, so these angles are complementary. When two angles sum to 90°, there's a direct relationship between their sine and cosine:

steps.title: Apply the complementary angle identity
steps.content: Since $R + S = 90°$, we have $S = 90° - R$. Therefore: $$\sin(R) = \cos(90° - R) = \cos(S)$$ We're given that $\sin(R) = \frac{\sqrt{11}}{6}$, so immediately: $$\cos(S) = \frac{\sqrt{11}}{6}$$ That matches choice A — A is correct.
